title: USS Higgins destroyer loses power and propulsion for hours in Indo-Pacific
summary: The guided-missile destroyer USS Higgins (DDG-76) lost power and propulsion for several hours in the Indo-Pacific after an engineering casualty in its electrical system, leaving the ship 'helpless' with its 300-person crew.
contents:
## US Navy Destroyer Suffers Engineering Casualty

### Incident Details
The Arleigh Burke-class guided-missile destroyer USS Higgins (DDG-76) experienced a complete loss of power throughout the ship while operating in the Indo-Pacific region. Commander Matthew Comer, spokesperson for the US 7th Fleet, confirmed that the ship suffered an "engineering casualty."

### Duration and Impact
A defense official confirmed the power and propulsion outage lasted "several hours," during which the ship remained immobile and electronically blind. Former US Navy Captain Carl Schuster assessed the situation as leaving the ship "helpless, electronically blind and immobile," with only emergency diesel generators powering communications and air conditioning.

### Technical Assessment
Initial reports indicate an electrical malfunction that "may have produced sparking or smoke that ceased once technicians removed power." The Aegis combat system and electrically operated radars would have gone offline during the outage, leaving the destroyer unable to detect or engage threats.

### Current Status
Power and propulsion returned to full operation aboard the Higgins. The cause of the problem remains under investigation. The Navy did not specify the exact location within the Indo-Pacific Command's area of responsibility.

### Ship Profile
The USS Higgins, first commissioned in 1999, homeported in Yokosuka, Japan, carries a crew of approximately 300. The 505-foot-long vessel displaces more than 8,200 tons and carries the Aegis combat system with vertical launch tubes for Tomahawk and other missiles. The Arleigh Burke-class constitutes the backbone of the US Navy's surface fleet, with more than 70 in service.
